title: Penne ai quattro formaggi
subtitle: Penne with a four-cheese cream sauce
excerpt: Penne in a sauce of four melted cheeses (gorgonzola, parmesan, fontina, and mozzarella), loosened with cream and pasta water. The Italian answer to a rich cheese sauce, built by combining mild and sharp cheeses in a ratio that balances flavour against body.
sourceNotes: Based on the quattro formaggi pasta tradition documented in Giorgio Locatelli, 'Made in Italy' (2006) and Marcella Hazan, 'Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking' (1992). The specific four-cheese combination varies by region and cook; this version uses gorgonzola for sharpness, parmesan for depth, fontina for melt, and mozzarella for stretch.

content.text: The quattro formaggi combination is not fixed — different regions and different cooks use different combinations of four Italian cheeses, balanced between sharp and mild, hard and soft, melting and flavour-giving. The version here uses gorgonzola for its assertive blue-cheese flavour, parmesan for depth and saltiness, fontina for its excellent melt (it stays smooth rather than stringy), and mozzarella for stretch and mild creaminess. Cream loosens the sauce and gives it body.

The sauce is made in a separate pan from the pasta: the cheeses melt into warm cream at a low temperature, creating a smooth base that the pasta is tossed through. Working at too high a temperature turns the sauce grainy — the proteins in the cheese seize rather than melt smoothly.

items.prepNote: rind removed, diced small; gruyère as substitute

content.text: Heat {{double-cream}} of cream with {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a medium saucepan over the lowest heat. When warm and steaming, add {{gorgonzola}} gorgonzola, {{fontina}} fontina, and {{mozzarella}} mozzarella. Stir gently until fully melted and smooth — do not let the cream boil. The sauce should be thick enough to coat a spoon.

Cook {{penne}} of penne in well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a cup of pasta water. Drain and add to the cheese sauce pan over a low heat. Toss to coat, adding pasta water a tablespoon at a time if the sauce is too thick. Stir in half the {{parmesan}} parmesan. Divide between bowls, scatter the remaining parmesan, and finish with {{black-pepper-ground}} of black pepper.

items.cause: Cream too hot when cheeses were added
items.fix: Use the lowest heat possible. The cream should be warm, not simmering. If the sauce separates, take off the heat immediately and stir in a splash of warm pasta water.

items.cause: Too much blue cheese for the batch
items.fix: Use 50 g gorgonzola and increase the fontina or mozzarella to compensate. The sauce should taste of cheese broadly, not exclusively of blue cheese.

content.text: Pasta ai quattro formaggi appears across Italy without fixed regional ownership: it is a preparation rather than a local speciality. The combination of four cheeses varies from the north, where fontina and grana padano feature, to the south, where pecorino and mozzarella dominate. In Italian restaurants outside Italy it became a fixture of the menu from the 1990s, positioned between the lighter tomato-based sauces and the heavier meat preparations — a reliable option for those who find cream sauces appealing and blue cheese non-confrontational.
